proof_sketch: batch_size=128, grid_dims(batch_size) => blockIdx.x ranges 0..127. Original condition batch_idx>=batch_size is false for all blocks. Mutated batch_idx>batch_size is also false for all blocks. No block ever triggers the early return, so behavior identical.

reasoning_content: We need to analyze the mutation: original condition `batch_idx >= batch_size` changed to `batch_idx > batch_size`. The kernel is launched with grid_dims(batch_size) where batch_size is fixed at 128. blockIdx.x ranges from 0 to 127. In original, condition checks if batch_idx >= batch_size (i.e., >=128) then return. Since blockIdx.x never reaches 128, no block is skipped. In mutated, condition checks if batch_idx > batch_size (i.e., >128), which is also never true. So both conditions are always false for all blocks. Therefore the mutation has no effect; it's equivalent under fixed shape. The mutant is unkillable under the fixed-shape contract. The tests all passed because indeed no difference exists. So we must conclude killable: false, reason_category: predicate_unreachable. The proof sketch: blockIdx.x in [0,127], batch_size=128, so condition never triggers. Survival reason: the boundary guard is tightened but the guard is unreachable because blockIdx.x is always less than batch_size. No value-level test can make blockIdx.x equal to 128. So unkillable. Suggested_test null.
